We retrospectively analysed 1 860 wound images acquired during routine clinical practice and independently annotated by four expert clinicians. An automated image\u2010analysis pipeline was used to segment the wound, standardise the peri\u2010wound border region, and estimate the three\u2010dimensional profile of the wound edge. We first tested whether geometry\u2010derived edge profiles alone could reproduce clinical wound edge categories. We then evaluated whether adding global visual descriptors of wound shape, colour appearance, and surface pattern improved agreement with clinicians. Inter\u2010clinician agreement was low, confirming the intrinsic subjectivity of wound edge classification. Geometry\u2010based analysis identified coherent edge\u2010profile patterns but showed poor correspondence with clinical annotations. In contrast, a supervised classifier incorporating both geometric and visual features achieved agreement comparable to, and in some comparisons higher than, the agreement observed among clinicians. Clinical wound edge assessment is not driven by edge geometry alone. Visual cues such as wound shape, colour appearance, and surface pattern appear to influence expert classification and may contribute to variability. Automated image\u2010based analysis may support more reproducible wound edge assessment, provided that it is externally validated in diverse clinical settings.<\/jats:p>","DOI":"10.1111\/exd.70287","type":"journal-article","created":{"date-parts":[[2026,6,6]],"date-time":"2026-06-06T06:45:01Z","timestamp":1780728301000},"update-policy":"https:\/\/doi.org\/10.1002\/crossmark_policy","source":"Crossref","is-referenced-by-count":0,"title":["Toward
